fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a text layout engine library built in Rust. We've been getting complaints from users that rendering mixed-direction text, handling cursor positions, and doing paragraph wrapping all require tons of custom glue code that keeps breaking. The idea is to wrap the parley/fontique ecosystem into something clean that apps can just call into.

Basically it needs to handle the usual stuff — laying out lines, measuring how wide content is before committing to a layout, embedding little boxes inside text (like icons or images inline), controlling how lines wrap when text overflows, aligning text left/right/center, and inspecting individual character clusters for hit testing and cursor logic. Also line height control — we had a bug last quarter where the absolute spacing mode wasn't being respected, similar to how we fixed the metric scaling in that other rendering module.

The adapter layer that reads commands and spits out results should be totally separate from the core logic — we don't want the business rules tangled up with the JSON parsing like what happened last time. There should be a test harness that runs all the case files and saves raw output per case so we can diff them. Cases live under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and output should go under rcb_tests/stdout/ namespaced by the cases directory name. Needs to match the expected output exactly.

One quick follow-up since a couple details came up while people were wiring the harness and checking outputs. For the saved stdout artifacts, the filename format needs to be `{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt`. So for example, the first case (index 0) in `feature1_multiline_layout.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` should land at `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_multiline_layout@000.txt`. The case index is zero-padded to 3 digits. Also, `rcb_tests/test.sh` takes an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` flag, and if you don’t pass it the default is `test_cases` (not `public_test_cases`). If you want the public suite specifically, you do need to pass `--cases-dir public_test_cases`.

Also want to be extra explicit on the separation line here: the core layout logic needs to stay completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The adapter should be a dedicated execution adapter in a physically separate file/module whose only job is deserializing JSON input into typed structs and calling idiomatic methods on the core domain. The core must never import JSON or I/O libraries. That’s the Dependency Inversion Principle version of this: high-level layout rules depend on abstractions, not I/O details. Along the same lines, each `.txt` file in `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/` should contain ONLY the raw stdout from the program under test — no PASS/FAIL labels, no metadata, no headers — so we can do direct byte-for-byte comparison against the `expected_output` field in the JSON case file.

For the line range question, yes: when text contains explicit newline characters (`\n`), the line range for that line includes the newline byte. For example, `"Hello world!\nLine 2\nLine 4"` produces line[0] range 0..13 (includes the `\n` at index 12), and line[1] range 13..20 (includes the `\n` at index 19). The final line has no trailing newline so its range ends exactly at the last character byte.

And for the text dump format, the `layout_summary` action outputs: `direction=<ltr|rtl>`, `line_count=<n>`, `width=<f>`, `full_width=<f>`, `height=<f>`, then for each line: `line[i]=range:<s>..<e> advance:<f> trailing:<f> offset:<f> baseline:<f> break:<reason> items:<n>`, followed by each run: `run[i.j]=range:<s>..<e> dir:<ltr|rtl> clusters:<n> advance:<f>`. All floats are formatted to exactly 2 decimal places.

Last thing, on the line height bug we called out before: the `line_height` feature (Feature 7) with `mode: absolute` must set baselines at `line_height_value * (line_index + 0.75)` effectively. So for absolute 20.0 the baselines are 15.00 and 35.00, matching the parley LineHeight::Absolute configuration path in the layout engine core.

trigger_keywords: what does the inline_boxes action output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `inline_boxes` action outputs `line_count=<n>` first, then interleaved records in visual line order: glyph runs as `glyph_run[line=<i>]=range:<s>..<e> dir:<ltr|rtl> offset:<f> baseline:<f> advance:<f> glyphs:<n>` and boxes as `box[line=<i>]=id:<id> x:<f> y:<f> width:<f> height:<f>`. All floats are 2 decimal places.

The `content_widths` action outputs exactly two lines: `min_width=<f>` and `max_width=<f>`, each float rounded to 2 decimal places. The min width is the longest unbreakable segment; the max width is the longest line ignoring soft wraps.

The `wrap_summary` action outputs `line_count=<n>` followed by one record per line: `line[i]=range:<s>..<e> advance:<f> break:<reason>`. Break reasons include `Regular`, `Explicit`, and `None`. Floats are 2 decimal places.

The `cluster_flags` action emits one record per cluster: `cluster[<line>.<run>.<idx>]=text:"<escaped>" range:<s>..<e> dir:<ltr|rtl> word_boundary:<bool> soft_break:<bool> hard_break:<bool> line_break:<None|Some(Explicit)|Some(None)> start:<bool> end:<bool> ligature_start:<bool> ligature_continuation:<bool> glyphs:<n> advance:<f>`. The `line_break` field uses Rust Option syntax: `None` when no break context, `Some(Explicit)` for hard breaks, `Some(None)` for the final line's non-break status.

trigger_keywords: what modes are supported for line_height
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `line_height` object in the JSON input accepts a `mode` field with values: `absolute` (fixed pixel height per line box), `em` or font-size-relative (scales from the active font size), and metric-relative (uses font metrics). For `absolute` mode with value 20.0 and two lines, total height is 40.00 and baselines fall at 15.00 and 35.00.

trigger_keywords: how is center alignment offset computed
start end center justify offset field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The per-line `offset` field in `layout_summary` reflects alignment. For `center` alignment with container width W and line advance A, offset = (W - A) / 2. For the test case with width=200.0: line[0] advance=84.32 → offset=(200-84.32)/2=57.84; line[1] advance=42.74 → offset=(200-42.74)/2=78.63. For `start` alignment all offsets are 0.00. Direction-aware `end` alignment sets offset to (W - A) for ltr paragraphs.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'layout_summary', 'inline_boxes', 'content_widths', 'wrap_summary', 'cluster_flags'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: like what happened last time with the JSON parsing tangled up
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The adapter module (e.g., `src/adapter.rs` or `adapter/mod.rs`) must be physically and logically separate from the core layout module (e.g., `src/layout/` or `src/engine.rs`). The JSON deserialization crate (serde_json) should only appear as a dependency of the adapter, not the core.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. Please check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the exact expected output format, and refer to the architecture constraints in the PRD about separation of concerns between the adapter layer and the core layout engine.
